description: Canonical behavioral definitions and constants shared by the TypeScript Android runtime (@chatsense/core) and the Python research/reference package (python/chatsense_ml/). This JSON is the single source of truth. Both languages load it through a thin constants layer (packages/chatsense-core/src/contract.ts and chatsense_ml/contract.py) whose values are proven equal to this file by parity tests. The Android app does NOT bundle Python; this file is the only thing both implementations share.

relationship_dynamics.turn_definition: A new turn begins at the first valid message, when the sender changes, or when the gap reaches thread_gap_min.
relationship_dynamics.reconnection_definition: A reconnection is the first message after a gap greater than or equal to reconnection_gap_min.
relationship_dynamics.follow_up_definition: A follow-up before reply occurs when the same participant sends again before anyone else responds, at least follow_up_min minutes have passed, and the gap remains below thread_gap_min.
relationship_dynamics.notes: Relationship dynamics are deterministic descriptive summaries of exported timing and volume behavior. They do not inspect message meaning and must not be represented as proof of motive, affection, attachment, personality, mental health, relationship quality, or relationship status. Reconnection-share changes use a documented 20 percentage-point notable threshold because reconnections are sparse by construction and already require at least two reconnection events in each compared period.

takeaway.safety_line: Observed in this export; it does not explain why.
takeaway.notes: Stage 6.2 human takeaways translate the existing narrative into plain orientation without adding behavioral scores. Stage 6.4 keeps the compact safety line export-scoped and non-causal while the full guardrail remains in detailed sections. A read is 'strong' only when the supporting event count reaches strong_evidence_multiplier times the corresponding maintenance/sample minimum; limited evidence always produces a 'limited' read. Confidence labels are product copy, not statistical claims.

insight_narrative.required_guardrail: These observations describe exported timing and volume only. They do not prove motive, love, rejection, affection, attachment, personality, mental health, relationship quality, or relationship status.
insight_narrative.notes: The narrative is a deterministic presentation of existing behavioral metrics. Every finding must carry visible evidence, comparison findings must use sufficient evidence and canonical notable-change thresholds, and limited evidence must be stated rather than filled with speculation. A maintenance imbalance requires a participant share at or above uneven_share_min_pct with its corresponding sample minimum. Balanced volume requires both top message share and top turn share at or below balanced_max_top_share_pct. priority_order documents the Overview lead order: an evidence-backed uneven-maintenance finding outranks notable changes; a limited 'no strong comparison' card must never outrank a maintenance finding that carries real evidence. The narrative does not inspect message content and must not contain live forecasts, coaching, advice, or generated interpretations.

carried_contact.notes: Asymmetry labels reuse balanced_max_top_share_pct (balanced band) and uneven_share_min_pct (lean threshold) from insight_narrative.maintenance_thresholds, plus high_follow_up_rate_pct for the follow-up construct. Message or turn volume alone can never produce an asymmetric label; at least min_agreeing_constructs contact-keeping constructs must agree.

relationship_read.notes: Stage 8A hero-read presentation thresholds. This is a TypeScript-only mapping layer over shipped metrics: no shared behavioral math changes, so the Python reference package does not mirror this section. Comparable quiet stretches are completed inter-message gaps of at least reconnection_gap_min. The ongoing quiet (device time minus the export's last message) is right-censored: it is ranked against completed comparable pauses but never enters its own reference distribution and is never described as completed. A historical-next-pattern sentence requires at least next_pattern_min_completed_pauses comparable completed pauses and is suppressed in code when at least estrangement_dominance_min_share_pct percent of comparable pauses ran estrangement_pause_min_days days or longer.

reply_event.notes: A reply event exists at index i>=1 when message[i].sender != message[i-1].sender. Consecutive messages from the same sender are NOT replies and never create an artificial reply edge or reply-delay sample.

reply_graph.to: previous sender (the one being replied to)
reply_graph.notes: Directed edge weight is the number of reply events from `from` to `to`.

date_order_policy.rule: Scan all timestamped lines in the export in order. If any line's first date component is > 12, the whole export is day/month/year (DMY). Otherwise, if any line's second date component is > 12, the whole export is month/day/year (MDY). Otherwise default to DMY. The single inferred order is then applied to every row; the order is never decided per row.

silence_anomaly.name: runtime_modified_zscore_floored
silence_anomaly.formula: threshold = max(thread_gap_min, median(gaps) + k * scale * MAD(gaps)); a gap is an unusual silence when gap > threshold; MAD = median(|gap - median(gaps)|).
silence_anomaly.notes: This is the SHIPPED runtime silence-anomaly definition computed by both @chatsense/core and chatsense_ml core silence_metrics. The Python research module chatsense_ml/anomaly/silence_anomaly.py exposes a DIFFERENT, unfloored, configurable-z research variant under the distinct name `research_silence_zscore`; it is explicitly not the runtime metric.

message_types.system_handling: System notice lines (timestamped lines with no 'Sender:' prefix) are detected. They are EXCLUDED from shared runtime behavioral parity metrics (participant counts, reply dynamics, balance, initiation, activity, graph). The Python reference report may retain them for research, but the cross-language parity contract is defined over non-system messages only.

rounding.notes: The TypeScript runtime already emits these metrics as integers (rates as integer percent, delays/longest-silence as integer minutes via Math.round). The Python parity normalizer applies the identical round-half-up at the boundary, so every shared metric is an integer or categorical value and all comparisons use EXACT equality. No aggressive rounding is introduced beyond what the shipped runtime already produces. The unusual-silence COUNT is the shared silence-anomaly output; the raw threshold value is an internal quantity (sensitive to the runtime's integer-rounded median/MAD) and is intentionally not compared, while both sides compute it from the same contract constants (k, scale, floor).

group_chat.limitations: The reply model is pairwise (reply = previous sender differs). In group chats with 3+ participants this attributes each message as a reply to the immediately preceding sender only; it does not resolve threaded @-mentions or quoted replies. Initiation and reply-graph metrics inherit this pairwise approximation. Treat group-chat reply attribution as an approximation, not ground truth.
